perturbation.original_text: Prior to disclosing the Confidential Information to any Representative, however, the Recipient shall inform the Representative of the confidential nature of such information and undertake reasonable efforts to cause them to treat such information on a confidential basis. The Recipient shall be responsible for the breach of this Agreement by its Representatives that have not separately signed a non-disclosure agreement with the Disclosing Party or any of its Representatives, and shall take all reasonable measures to restrain its Representatives from unauthorized disclosure of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Prior to disclosing the Confidential Information to any Representative, however, the Recipient should consider informing the Representative of the confidential nature of such information and may undertake efforts to encourage them to treat such information on a confidential basis. The Recipient shall be responsible for the breach of this Agreement by its Representatives that have not separately signed a non-disclosure agreement with the Disclosing Party or any of its Representatives, and shall attempt to restrain its Representatives from unauthorized disclosure of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall inform" to "should consider informing" and "undertake reasonable efforts to cause" to "may undertake efforts to encourage" weakens the obligation. Replacing "take all reasonable measures to restrain" with "attempt to restrain" introduces ambiguity and reduces the standard of care required.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires a higher standard of care to protect trade secrets. The modified text's weaker language suggests a lower level of effort is acceptable, potentially failing to meet the requirement of 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy, which can be interpreted as more than simply 'attempting' or 'encouraging'.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The original contract language mandates specific actions ('shall inform', 'undertake reasonable efforts', 'take all reasonable measures'), while the changed text weakens these obligations using softer language ('should consider informing', 'may undertake efforts', 'attempt to restrain'). The LLM explanation points out that CUTSA requires a 'reasonable efforts' standard to protect trade secrets. The weakened language in the modified contract suggests a lower standard of care, potentially failing to meet the 'reasonable efforts' requirement. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'improper means' under California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) to include 'breach of a duty to maintain secrecy.' By weakening the recipient's obligation to maintain confidentiality, the changed text increases the risk of a breach and thus contradicts the intent of CUTSA, creating an ambiguous legal obligation. The weakening of the original text to be more discretionary creates a contradiction of the standard of care required.

perturbation.original_text: Accordingly, each party agrees that without the prior written consent of the other, it will not, and it will direct its Representatives not to, disclose to any person, other than its Representatives, either the fact that discussions or negotiations are taking place concerning the Proposed Transaction between IDT and PLX or any of the terms and conditions with respect to the Proposed Transaction and no Confidential Information will be provided to such persons.
perturbation.changed_text: Accordingly, each party agrees that without what they consider to be prior written consent of the other, it intends not to, and it will advise its Representatives not to, disclose to any person, other than its Representatives, either the fact that discussions or negotiations are taking place concerning the Proposed Transaction between IDT and PLX or any of the general terms and conditions with respect to the Proposed Transaction and no highly sensitive Confidential Information will be provided to such persons.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "prior written consent" to "what they consider to be prior written consent" allows subjective interpretation. Changing 'it will not' to 'it intends not to' suggests a lack of firm commitment. Replacing 'any of the terms and conditions' with 'any of the general terms and conditions' creates ambiguity. Replacing 'no Confidential Information' with 'no highly sensitive Confidential Information' introduces a subjective classification of information, allowing for interpretation of what is "highly sensitive".
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, specifically insider trading regulations
perturbation.law_explanation: The modifications weaken the restriction on disclosing information related to the proposed transaction. Even disclosing general terms could be a violation of insider trading rules if that information is material and non-public. The subjective interpretation of "prior written consent" and "highly sensitive" creates loopholes that could be exploited to share information illegally.

Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguities that could lead to non-compliance with insider trading rules, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 (15 USC 78j) broadly prohibits manipulative and deceptive devices in securities trading, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation explain how the changes weaken restrictions on disclosing information and create loopholes. The original text had clear restrictions (prior written consent, it will not disclose, any of the terms and conditions, no Confidential Information). The modified text uses ambiguous language (what they consider to be prior written consent, it intends not to, any of the general terms and conditions, no highly sensitive Confidential Information) which undermines the original intent and creates potential for illegal information sharing.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the Disclosing Party’s written request, the Recipient shall promptly destroy or return to the Disclosing Party all written Confidential Information (whether prepared by the Disclosing party or its Representatives) and will not retain any copies, extracts or other reproductions in whole or in part of such written materials except that (i) one copy of each electronic version of such materials may be retained by Recipient and its Representatives in their archives pursuant to such party’s standard data backup and disaster recovery plans and (ii) Recipient’s outside legal counsel and accountants may retain one hard copy of such materials in its archives solely for regulatory or corporate records retention policy compliance and for dispute resolution; provided, however, that any Confidential Information contained in such format may not be used for any other purpose and that all obligations contained herein shall, with respect to any Confidential Information so retained, survive any termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the Disclosing Party’s written request, the Recipient shall endeavor to destroy or return to the Disclosing Party all written Confidential Information (whether prepared by the Disclosing party or its Representatives) and will try not to retain any copies, extracts or other reproductions in whole or in part of such written materials except that (i) one copy of each electronic version of such materials may be retained by Recipient and its Representatives in their archives pursuant to what they consider to be reasonable data backup and disaster recovery plans and (ii) Recipient’s outside legal counsel and accountants may retain one hard copy of such materials in its archives for regulatory or corporate records retention policy compliance and for potential dispute resolution; provided, however, that any Confidential Information contained in such format will be used in a manner that is ethical and that all obligations contained herein may, with respect to any Confidential Information so retained, survive any termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "shall promptly destroy" with "shall endeavor to destroy" and "will not retain" with "will try not to retain" weakens the commitment. Replacing "standard data backup and disaster recovery plans" with "what they consider to be reasonable data backup and disaster recovery plans" allows for subjective interpretation. Removing "solely" from the purpose of retaining data broadens the allowed uses. Replacing "may not be used for any other purpose" with "will be used in a manner that is ethical" makes the restriction highly subjective and difficult to enforce. Replacing 'shall' with 'may' on the obligations surviving termination weakens commitment.
perturbation.law_explanation: The CCPA grants consumers the right to request deletion of their personal information. By weakening the obligation to destroy or return confidential information and allowing for subjective interpretation of data retention practices, the modified text introduces ambiguity that could conflict with the CCPA's mandate to comply with consumer deletion requests. The 'ethical manner' clause is insufficient to ensure compliance with data minimization principles.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1798.105 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.81.5. California Consumer Privacy Act of 2018 [1798.100 - 1798.199.100] ( Title 1.81.5 added by Stats. 2018, Ch. 55, Sec. 3. ) 1798.105. Consumers’ Right to Delete Personal Information (a) A consumer shall have the right to request that a business delete any personal information about the consumer which the business has collected from the consumer. (b) A business that collects personal information about consumers shall disclose, pursuant to Section 1798.130, the consumer’s rights to request the deletion of the consumer’s personal information. (c) (1) A business that receives a verifiable consumer request from a consumer to delete the consumer’s personal information pursuant to subdivision (a) of this section shall delete the consumer’s personal information from its records, notify any service providers or contractors to delete the consumer’s personal information from their records, and notify all third parties to whom the business has sold or shared the personal information to delete the consumer’s personal information unless this proves impossible
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: ck the business’s privacy policy to make sure you submitted your request through the designated way. Follow up with the business to see if the business is subject to the CCPA and to follow up on your request. 4. Why is the business asking me for more information? Businesses must verify that the person making a request to delete is the consumer about whom the business has personal information. Businesses may need to ask you for additional information for verification purposes. If the business asks for personal information to verify your identity, it can only use that information for this verification purpose. 5. Why did the business deny my request to delete? There are exceptions to the right to delete. Common reasons why businesses may keep your personal information include: If the information is exempt from the CCPA. This includes: Publicly available information (such as your address, which is often in public real estate/property records) . However, if you are a law enforcement officer, public official, or Safe at Home participant (available to victims of domestic violence, stalking, sexual assault, human trafficking, elder and dependent abuse, as well as reproductive health workers), you may request a website to not publicly post your address as described here . Certain types of information such as medical information or consumer credit reporting information. The business cannot verify your request To complete your transaction, provide a reasonably anticipated product or service, or for certain warranty and product recall purposes For certain business security practices For certain internal uses that are compatible with reasonable consumer expectations or the context in which the information was provided To comply with legal obligations, exercise legal claims or rights, or defend legal claims If the personal information is certain medical information, consumer credit reporting information, or other types of information exempt from the CCPA See Civil Code sections 1798.105(d) and 1798.145 for more exceptions. If you do not know why a business denied your request to delete, follow up with the business to ask it for its reasons. 6. Why did I get a response that the business is a service provider that does not have to act on my request? Many businesses use other businesses to provide services for them. For example, a retailer may contract with a payment card processor to process customer credit card transactions or a shipping company to deliver orders. These entities may qualify as “service providers” under the CCPA. The CCPA treats service providers differently than the businesses they serve. It is the business that is responsible for responding to consumer requests. If you submit a request to delete to a service provider of a business instead of the business itself, the service provider may deny the request. You must submit your request to the business itself. If a service provider has said that it does not or cannot act on your request because it is a service provider, you may follow up to ask who the business is. However, sometimes the service provider will not be able to provide that information. You may be able to determine who the business is based on the services that the service provider provides, although sometimes this may be difficult or impossible. 7. Why is a debt collector still calling me about my debt even though I asked it to delete my information? Creditors, collection agencies, and other debt collectors can still try to collect debts that you owe even if you asked them to delete your personal information. Learn more about debt collectors—including what they can and can’t do— here . 8. Why is a credit reporting agency still giving out my credit information even though I asked it to delete my information? Credit reporting agencies like Equifax, Experian, and TransUnion can still collect and disclose your credit information, subject to regulation under the Fair Credit Reporting Act . Learn more about your rights under t
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguities that weaken consumer data protection, potentially conflicting with CCPA. The original text uses stronger language like "shall promptly destroy" and "will not retain", while the changed text uses weaker terms like "shall endeavor to destroy" and "will try not to retain," introducing uncertainty. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically section 1798.105(a), grants consumers the right to request deletion of their personal information. The modified text's allowance for subjective interpretation of data retention practices and the replacement of "may not be used for any other purpose" with "will be used in a manner that is ethical" creates loopholes that could undermine the consumer's right to have their data deleted as required by the CCPA.

perturbation.original_text: In consideration of the Confidential Information being furnished hereby, each party agrees that, without the prior written consent of the Chief Financial Officer or General Counsel of the other party, for a period of twelve (12) months from the date of this Agreement neither it nor any of its affiliates will, either directly or indirectly, solicit for employment, or otherwise contract for the services of (or cause or seek to cause to leave the employ of other party or any of its affiliates) any person who is now employed or engaged (either as an employee or consultant) or becomes employed or engaged during the term of this Agreement by the other party or its affiliates, other than persons whose employment or engagement shall have been terminated at least six (6) months prior to the date of such solicitation, employment or other contractual arrangements.
perturbation.changed_text: In consideration of the Confidential Information being furnished hereby, each party agrees that, absent a good faith effort to obtain prior written consent of the Chief Financial Officer or General Counsel of the other party, for a period of what they consider to be approximately twelve (12) months from the date of this Agreement neither it nor any of its affiliates will, to the best of their understanding, either directly or indirectly, solicit for regular employment, or otherwise contract for vaguely similar services of (or encourage, without directly causing or seeking to cause to leave the employ of other party or any of its affiliates) any person who is currently employed or engaged (either as a full-time employee or long-term consultant) or may become employed or engaged during a reasonable term of this Agreement by the other party or its affiliates, other than persons whose prior employment or engagement shall have been terminated sometime around six (6) months prior to the date of such solicitation, employment or other contractual arrangements.
perturbation.explanation: The changes throughout introduce subjective interpretations and weaken the restrictions. 'Absent a good faith effort' introduces a subjective standard. 'What they consider to be approximately twelve (12) months' introduces ambiguity regarding the duration. 'To the best of their understanding' weakens the commitment. 'Solicit for regular employment' narrows the scope of the restriction. 'Vaguely similar services' introduces ambiguity. 'Encourage, without directly causing' creates a loophole. 'Currently' and 'full-time' narrow the scope. 'Reasonable term' is subjective. 'Sometime around six (6) months' is vague.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 16600 generally prohibits non-compete agreements in California. While the original text is a non-solicitation clause, the numerous ambiguities introduced weaken the protection afforded to employees and make it more likely that a party could circumvent the intent of the clause, potentially violating the spirit of the law by unduly restricting an employee's ability to seek employment. The numerous ambiguities create loopholes allowing for actions that could effectively restrain trade.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities that weaken the original non-solicitation clause, making it more difficult to enforce and potentially circumventing the intent of the law, as described in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically BPC 16600(a), states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The ambiguities introduced, such as 'absent a good faith effort,' 'what they consider to be approximately twelve (12) months,' 'to the best of their understanding,' and 'vaguely similar services,' create loopholes. These changes make it less clear what is prohibited, which could be interpreted as a restraint on trade, thus contradicting the law.
